items.front: What equipment is normally required to operate in Class B airspace?
items.back: An operable two-way radio and the applicable transponder with altitude-reporting equipment.

items.front: What must a VFR aircraft obtain before operating in Class B airspace?
items.back: An ATC clearance to enter or operate in the Bravo airspace.

items.front: What is the main Class B routing goal for large turbine aircraft using the primary airport?
items.back: To the extent practical, route them to avoid VFR corridors and airspace below the Class B floor where VFR aircraft are operating.

items.front: How are VFR aircraft separated from aircraft weighing more than 19,000 pounds or from turbojets in Class B service?
items.back: By at least 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical separation, or visual separation.

items.front: What traffic-advisory rule applies in Class B service?
items.back: Provide mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts between all aircraft, and apply merging-target procedures.

items.front: What helicopter and powered-lift separation exception applies in Class B service?
items.back: VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft do not need separation from VFR or IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft, but traffic advisories and safety alerts still apply.

items.front: When must a VFR aircraft in Class B be told to resume appropriate VFR altitudes?
items.back: When the contrary altitude assignment is no longer required or when the aircraft leaves Class B airspace.

items.front: Who specifies the approach interval in Class B service?
items.back: The tower specifies the approach interval.
